1: 'Send the tribute lamb to the ruler of the land, dispatched from Sela across the desert to the mount of Daughter Zion' — the opening of Isaiah 16 responds to the disaster of Isaiah 15: the advice to Moab is to submit, send tribute, and seek shelter from Zion. 'Send a tribute lamb to the ruler of the land' (shilchu-kar moshlei erets misela, send a tribute-lamb to the ruler of the land from Sela) — kar (lamb) was a standard tribute commodity in the ancient Near East. The Mesha Stele records that Mesha of Moab paid tribute to Israel's king of 100,000 lambs and 100,000 rams' wool (2 Kings 3:4). 'From Sela' (misela, from the rock/Sela) — either a Moabite city name ('the rock') or the fortress city of Petra in Edom (if Moabite refugees fled south). The tribute goes to 'Daughter Zion' (bat-Tsiyon, the personified Jerusalem) — submit to YHWH's city rather than trust in national pride.
2: 'The women of Moab will be like a fluttering bird, like scattered nestlings, stranded at the fords of the Arnon' — the refugee image: Moabite women as a fluttering bird (of tsippor noded, like a wandering/flying bird) and 'scattered nestlings' (kaqqen nodad, like a nest scattered/overthrown). The Arnon River (modern Wadi Mujib) was Moab's northern boundary — the fords across the Arnon were the crossing point between Moabite and Israelite territory. The image is of panicked birds displaced from their nest — the ordinary settled life of Moab's population disrupted by the violence of conquest. The women specifically are highlighted — the most vulnerable in military conquest.
3: 'Offer counsel, dispense justice; make your shade as dark as midnight at midday; shelter the refugees; do not betray the one who is fleeing' — the appeal to Judah (or possibly to the Moabites themselves urging cooperation with Judah): four imperatives: bring counsel (havi'u etsah, bring counsel/advice), dispense justice (asu mishpat, execute justice), make your shade/shadow like the night (shiti chalilah tsillekh betoch-tsaharayim, make your shade like night at noon — provide complete shelter even in daytime). 'Do not betray the one who is fleeing' (al-tegallit noded) — the covenant of asylum that protects refugees. The language of asylum and sheltering the fugitive reflects the ANE custom of sanctuary — even enemies could claim protection in certain sacred contexts.
4: 'Let my displaced people take shelter with you; be a refuge for them from the ravager. For the tyrant is gone, destruction is over, and the one who trampled is out of the land' — the appeal for refuge: 'let my displaced people dwell in you' (yaguru vekha niddachai Moav, let the outcasts of Moab sojourn with you) — the Moabite refugees taking shelter in Judah. This situation may have a specific historical referent: Moabite refugees fleeing to Judah during Assyrian campaigns. The eschatological dimension: 'for the tyrant (mits) is gone, destruction (shadud) is over, the trampler (roames) has ceased out of the land' — the oppressor has ended and shelter is now possible. This may anticipate the post-Assyrian/Babylonian restoration when the immediate military threat is past.
5: 'Then a throne will be set up in covenant faithfulness; one will sit on it in truth in the tent of David — one who judges and actively pursues justice and is swift to act with righteousness' — the messianic throne promise embedded in the Moab oracle: 'vekon bechesed kise, then a throne will be established in loyal-love/covenant-faithfulness.' The throne set up in chesed (the covenantal love/loyalty of the Davidic covenant, 2 Samuel 7:15: 'my lovingkindness [chesed] will never be taken away from him') and veeshav alav be'emet (one will sit on it in truth/faithfulness). 'In the tent of David' (be'ohel David) — the tabernacle/dwelling of David, the Davidic dynasty's enduring promise. The specific qualities: judging (shofet), pursuing justice (mevaqesh mishpat), swift to righteousness (mehir tsedaqah). Amos 9:11 also promises the restoration of 'David's fallen tent.' Acts 15:16-17 cites this in the context of Gentile inclusion — the tent of David restored to include all nations.
6: 'We have heard of Moab's arrogance — his immense pride, his haughtiness and conceit and fury — his empty boasting amounts to nothing' — the grounds for Moab's judgment stated plainly: shama'nu ge'on-Moav ge'eh meod (we have heard of Moab's pride — he is very proud), his gaavah (arrogance), gee'ut (pride/haughtiness), evra (overflow of anger/fury), and his baddav lo-ken (his empty boastings — baddim = empty/vain things, threads = empty boasts). Moab's specific sin is pride — the same sin as Babylon (Isaiah 13-14) but in a smaller nation. The Moabite national pride recorded in the Mesha Stele ('Chemosh has given me victory over Israel') is the background for this charge of divine-dismissing self-confidence.
7: 'So let Moab wail over Moab — let everyone wail; mourn, utterly stricken, over the loss of Kir-hareseth' — the universal mourning commanded: yeylelil Moav leMenua, let Moab wail for Moab (the nation mourning for itself). The specific lament for Kir-hareseth (the great fortified city of Moab, modern Kerak — a massive hilltop fortress that controlled the south Moabite plateau). The phrase 'ashishei Kir-chareseth tahegas umadovim yileku (for the raisin-cakes of Kir-hareseth you will be stricken, altogether devastated)' — the specific agricultural product (raisin-cakes, ashishot, pressed grape-cakes used in feasting and possibly cult worship) of the region destroyed. The specificity grounds the mourning in concrete economic and cultural loss.
8: 'For the terraces of Heshbon have withered; the vine of Sibmah — whose branches once stretched as far as Jazer, wandered through the desert, and reached to the sea — its finest vines have been struck down by the rulers of the nations' — the vine of Sibmah (the famous wine-producing area of Moab, northwest of Heshbon) is one of the great OT images of prosperity destroyed. The vine's extraordinary extent is described: reaching to Jazer (north), wandering through the desert (east), reaching to the sea (the Dead Sea, west) — the vine covered the entire Moabite plateau in its prime. Jeremiah 48:32 quotes this verse almost verbatim in his Moab oracle ('I weep for you, O vine of Sibmah, more than I weep for Jazer'), showing the influence of Isaiah's Moab oracle on later prophetic tradition. The 'rulers of the nations' who cut down the vines = Assyrian or Babylonian military campaigns that stripped the productive land.
9: 'I weep for the vine of Sibmah with the tears of Jazer; I drench you with my own grief, O Heshbon and Elealeh — because the war cry has crashed down over your summer harvest and ripe grapes' — the prophet's direct personal mourning resumed (cf. Isaiah 15:5): 'al-ken evkeh bivchi Yazer gehen Sivmah, therefore I weep with the weeping of Jazer for the vine of Sibmah.' The prophet soaks Heshbon and Elealeh with his tears (artivekh, I will drench you). The specific agricultural loss: the harvest shout (heydad, the joy-cry of the harvest) has fallen on the summer fruit (qayits) and the vintage (batsir). The harvest shout is the sound of agricultural celebration (Jeremiah 25:30 uses it for divine judgment: YHWH shouting a heydad against his vineyard). The joy-cry of the harvest has become the cry of destruction.
10: 'Gladness and joy have been stripped from the orchards; in the vineyards no one sings, no one shouts for joy; no one treads grapes in the winepresses. I have silenced the harvest songs' — the negation of harvest celebration: 'gladness and joy' (simcha vegil) 'have been stripped' (ne'esaf, gathered away — the harvest verb ironically applied to the stripping of joy). The vineyard-silence contrasts with the normal harvest scenes of Psalms 65:11-13 (the pastures clothed with flocks, the valleys covered with grain, shouting and singing). 'No one treads grapes in the winepresses' (lo yidrok hayoqev) — the wine-pressing tread was communal celebration, accompanied by singing (Jeremiah 25:10: 'I will banish the sounds of joy and gladness'). 'I have silenced the harvest songs' (heydad hishbatti, I have caused the harvest-shout to cease).
11: 'My heart resonates like a lyre in mourning for Moab; my deepest self aches for Kir-hareseth' — the deepest expression of the prophet's grief: 'al-ken me'ai leMenua yehemdayun, therefore my bowels/inner parts sound like a lyre/harp for Moab.' The me'ayim (bowels/inner parts) were in Hebrew physiology the seat of deep emotion — the churning interior of grief sounds like the resonance of a lyre (kinnor = the lyre, the instrument of both joy and lament). The 'deepest self' (kirbi, my inner parts) aches for Kir-hareseth. The prophet's grief is visceral and musical — the body as a resonating instrument of compassion for the enemy nation's suffering. This is one of the OT's most remarkable expressions of prophetic empathy for a foreign people under divine judgment.
12: 'When Moab shows himself at the high place, wears himself out in prayer, and comes to his sanctuary to plead, he will not succeed' — the futility of Moab's cultic response: appearing at the high place (bamah, the local hilltop shrine), 'wearing himself out' (vnilah, and he will tire himself out) in worship, and coming to the sanctuary (miqdash, the holy place) to intercede (hitpallel, pray/intercede). But 'lo yukal' (he will not be able/succeed). Moab's god Chemosh (mentioned in the Mesha Stele; 1 Kgs 11:7 calls him the 'detestable god of Moab') cannot deliver what YHWH has decreed. The futility of pagan worship in the face of YHWH's judgment is a recurring prophetic theme (Isaiah 10:10-11; Isaiah 46:1-2). The specific futility of Moab's sanctuary worship connects to the contemptuous dismissal of Babylon's deities in Isaiah 40-48.
13: 'This is the word the LORD pronounced concerning Moab in the past' — the editorial note connecting the oracle to an earlier prophetic word (deborah, the older oracle). The phrase 'me'az' (from that time, in the past) indicates that what precedes (Isaiah 15:1-16:12) is an older prophetic tradition, and what follows (v14) is a fresh, more specific word with a timeline. This kind of editorial layering (older oracle + new specific application) is characteristic of the prophetic books — Jeremiah 48 similarly reworks the Isaiah Moab oracle with additions. The notation preserves the history of prophetic tradition while adding contemporary application.
14: 'But now the LORD declares: Within three years — no more than the term of a hired labourer — Moab's glory will be brought into contempt, for all its vast numbers; what is left will be very small and of no account' — the specific timeline: 'beshalosch shanim kishne sakir, in three years as the years of a hired laborer.' The hired laborer's term (sakir) was a fixed legal period — the comparison emphasizes exactness and certainty rather than vagueness. 'Within three years, no more' — 'lo yikkabed' (the glory will not be heavy/honored). Moab's 'vast numbers' (hamun rav, the great multitude) reduced to 'very small and of no account' (me'at mitsar). The time-specific oracle is a test of prophetic reliability (Deuteronomy 18:21-22: 'if a prophet speaks in YHWH's name but the thing does not happen — he spoke presumptuously'). The precision of '3 years like a hired worker's term' is a datable claim.
